commentBody: I was totally intrigued by the disconnect between the theme clues and theme answers, and never figured out the reason. That is my next job.
I enjoyed it!  Never thought I would be the first to comment, though.
Just waiting to read the first comment by someone whose nose is out of joint because of the mere inclusion of 43A, especially as clued.

Fine fit for a Wednesday. 
Nice job, Brian.


(Waiting to see if any ED staff will comment on the 8A answer.)
Clever and fun!

Fun puzzle with a great 'AHA' theme.  Though very much on the easy side for a Wednesday, way easier than yesterday.  Tuesday and Wednesday could have been reversed this week.

Some trickery with 1A having 'Alternative to Uber' not be 'Lyft'.

A couple clues I was lucky to get.  Like I've never heard of an ERMINE but I vaguely recall the lyric "Ermine fur" from a Dylan song.  Also knowing just enough Japanese to know what the Japanese word for dog is having no idea what a Shiba INU is.
If you knew Anubis, you’re not a Nooby, as I am.
8A should be included in the "Tricky Clues" section, since ER NURSES do triage, not the docs.
Nice.

Big few hours here for me. Mets won 10-2. Solved my first Midi with only across answers (I know my Mario!) And in the morning, Pete Wells article about me will be printed in the Food section of the Wednesday paper

My solve on Wednesdays is usually a waltzing around the puzzle, with downs and acrosses intermingled like steps as I go here and there around the grid's dance floor. 

For that reason, I had only halves of all the themers at first – PLUS, SYMBOL, OPEN, MARK, none of it making any sense. 

Then along comes the revealer and bingo! Now I could make my way back and complete all the themers. Ta-da! 

Because I solve on the phone, with the keyboard devoid of a shift key, I thought of how much more fun it would have been to solve this on a desktop. My loss.

The puzzle was great nonetheless. Imaginative, breezy, a perfect waltz.

Thank you, Mr. Keller. 
I look forward to more offerings from you!

P.S. Congrats on the 95% cluing acceptance. That's quite a feat!

Isaac, good column but there are FOUR theme answers plus a revealer, not three. 

Great puzzle, workin' the keyboard there. The AXON clue was gorgeous, and the Pirate Parrot makes an appearance. Once when my wife and I were vacationing during spring training in Florida, we caught a Pirates game and a particularly hot day. We went to the back to get a beer and a hotdog and my wife also got a bottle of water, and we headed back to our seats, only to be greeted by the Pirate Parrot, who stood towering over us, blocking our way. He pointed one of his wings to my wife's bottle of water.

"Hey Parrot," she said, sweetly. And gave him the bottle of water, which he twisted open with his claw and consumed in a single gulp. He bowed his great and noble head toward us in thanks, stepped around us, and was gone. I believe he was a replacement Parrot for the Parrot involved in the cocaine scandal that swept through the Buccos' dugout sometime around then, but I'm not sure. They may have kept him on despite the funny business.

Very clever theme and puzzle. I won’t say that it was tricky at all—I pretty much blew straight through it and got the theme on the second themer—but I still really enjoyed it, and glad I solve on the phone and couldn’t cheat. Also appreciate that two of the answers weren’t numbers, those were very artfully done. I also appreciate that even though many of the answers were gimmes for a Wednesday, there were several clues that impressed me and that I thought were really clever—AXONS, ORALB, SPIELS. Even Bulls and bucks—but for the capital letter you’d look for NBA teams. Plus I started the puzzle late and am exhausted so happy for something quick and fun today. Great work, two excellent sophomore efforts in a row—no sophomore slumps here!

I enjoyed the puzzle, and the theme gave me a good chuckle once I “clued” in. Thanks for the solve Brian!

I was disappointed in the Wordplay column today though. As mentioned in an earlier comment, there were in fact four theme clues, not three. On top of that, two of the four were not numbers. I usually really enjoy reading the column, but this one felt rushed and sloppy, and did not do the puzzle justice (the constructors note seemed longer than the article itself).

Adding to my earlier post... For those who don't know, Guns N' Roses lead guitarist, Saul Hudson, is known by his stage name Slash (= shift key + question mark). Here's my favorite song of theirs, "Patience" (yes, it's been confirmed, rock bands churn out some of the best and most iconic ballads!) https://youtu.be/ErvgV4P6Fzc?si=yfaKswMVFbFGFNpn

Would love to know the rationale behind the “swimwear line” clue replacement. “Kind of line at the beach” felt so clunky when solving, because what could that even be? A line for food? I jumped it immediately and went for the crossings, whereas the original would have gotten me thinking.
I understood what was going on but solving it on the phone I realised I didn't know what symbols or signs the SHIFT key allowed us to toggle between. Things we see everyday without it registering.

Using too many capital letters while typing is shifty behavior.

(I'm telling you just in case.)

I felt like a jumping jack until I was stopped cold in the NW corner. Guessing here, knowing there, wrong downs, a real cha-cha-cha of a puzzle, and fun to clean up as soon as I saw what I needed to do.  I still didn't see the true cleverness of it until it was done, which made it all the sweeter. 

Brian, I learned the qwerty in high school (flunked the class the first time and had to take it over, because there were disconnects in my brain and I kept doing things like typing an s instead of an l —something to do with the sneaky streak of left-handed-ness in our family. Never mind, in the end I was saved by the IBM Selectric, and typed my way to, well, not stardom, but to many a job.  

Your shifty puzzle was a joy to solve, Mr. Keller.  Thank you!

I solve on my phone so I basically had to brute-force the themed entries, as the clues were no help - I may be a computer touch typer, but I don't remember the exact layout of the keyboard. Going for a theme that really only works on some of the devices the crossword is available on seems like a poor idea.

I found the across fill very challenging, but the downs were Monday level. That being said, I'm still struggling with the NW corner - I haven't been able to fill it in yet. I'll give it a few more goes later. It's off to work for now.
Love a puzzle that actually makes me get off the phone for a minute and go look at something (a keyboard in this case!)

I thought I'd probably have to run a lot of the alphabet when two unknowns crossing and leaving blank square challenged me at 39D and 44A, but it only took two key presses! "TCBY" is vaguely familiar; I'll look it up later.

Oddly, it didn't take any kind of run to fill in the blanks of ANU__S at 57A though I didn't know the crosses until after I filled in the BI that suggested itself out of nowhere. My mind is starting to play that kind of trick on me.

+$?( =0.4444444444444444444444444

is SHIFTy arithmetic 🙂 

Harder on a phone keyboard. 

A cute puzzle. Thanks.
Nice puzzle! I feel much more comfortable with a Shift to the left than with Alt-right-control where the outcome is unpredictable, frequently disastrous and cannot be undone
Correct me if I’m wrong, but the animal which lends its fur is the STOAT, but the fur itself is an ERMINE😅
I enjoyed solving this shifty puzzle!  Even after I knew what the trick was, there was still some challenge and therefore some satisfaction at completing it.
Delightful puzzle, disappointing column.  Better than the other way around, to be fair.
Mr. Aranow's explanation of the theme clues is misleading.  Only two are numbers; the others are symbols.

I enjoyed this, even if I had to look up an image of a PC keyboard to refresh my memory of the keys. (No shift key on an IPad!) The only stumbling block for me was the NW corner, but once I sent the Lyft driver away I was fine. 

Still waiting for the WOKE firestorm. That was a bold entry. I approve!
Am I missing something? 45A “they’re adjusted when ‘truing’ a bicycle wheel” Disappointed at the typo
I'm visiting Barcelona right now. This week I learned that if 28D had said Barcelona rather than Seville, the answer would be TAPES.
Spelling 🐝  needs higher badges, especially for “Pangrams” and “Pangram First”.  Thanks for this great game!
Very fun theme!
fun puzzle, well done

I was doing so well, really enjoying this crossword, when - the bottom right corner. I've never had to use the word QUART for anything, definitely not ice cream. When milk came in bottles it was pints, then 2-pint cartons, now litres. But ice cream is just big or small tubs. 
Then TSA BIN was unknown and TCBY. That sounds quite a mouthful for a brand name! 
I must be the only person here who doesn't know about the name Slash.
However, still very enjoyable.

Stymied for a long time because I kept picturing a bar with MAPS of where the drinks are from. I thought it was odd, but it worked enough in my brain that I figured it was right. I originally put LYFT in for uber alternative, and when that was clearly not it, I thought MAXI was a good synonym for uber — something really, really extreme, to the max. 

TAXI and TAPS make so much more sense but now I like my map-filled bar…
I was worried when I figured out the revealer that I would get caught out by the differences between US and European keyboard layouts but luckily there was no need for at symbols or quotation marks!
Anyone else notice the 2D answer in a puzzle with a clue about Guns N' Roses? Different spelling, of course, but I wonder if it was intentional.

Nice one, whizzed through it contentedly. My keyboard is a bit different but that doesn't affect the ease of the solve.
I did "check" the ABBI/TCBY cross when the music didn't come at the end, never heard of either, but it turned out I'd just made a typo somewhere else.
Good, thanks Brian!
That was fun
Anyone else confused by 35-down? Personification of victory = NIKE. The running shoe? How is that a personification?
What a fun and clever theme! And any puzzle that has Slash as an answer is a Sweet Child of Mine!!!
The aha moment on the theme was after filling in PLUSSIGN from other answers and noticing the start of DOLLAR in 24A, looked at my keyboard and sure enough....  What a fun and clever theme!

A moment of silence for solvers with non-US keyboards. 

Luckily, I was solving on my work laptop, which I've specifically ordered with the US keyboard because it's just superior for programming.
I still remember the nightmare of trying to type my automatically assigned password (which was basically half symbols) on a French keyboard during my exchange. How did they end up with that layout??

Such a fun puzzle today anyway!

Just started it this morning. Here's how out of sync with it so far in the NW corner:
1A. LYFT and 20A. PAC  Any my typing dyslexia is at it's peak!
What's lurking for me in the rest of the grid??
Covfefe, anyone?

Wish we luck!

Why is QUART is one of my favorite words?

That’s a great question.
I’d love to tell you.

My dad was a quiet, stoic sort.
And he travelled for work, 
so we didn’t much time with him.

When I was eight,
he took us all out for ice cream.
Huge. Deal.

Standing in line, my dad
asked my older brothers 
( I’m the youngest of four )
if they knew why a quart was called a quart.

They shrugged,
but I lit up like a Christmas tree and asked,

“Is it because it’s a quarter of a gallon?”

The look of surprise on 
his face, my brother’s and sister’s faces…

Bliss.

Better than ice cream.

I floated home.


And, yeah.
I’m *totally* still talking about it 50 years later.

Bliss!
I enjoyed that theme.  I solve on my phone so I had to Google a picture of a keyboard.
Caught on at $.  Very clever theme and I also would have preferred Brian's original clues.  Well done and thanks.

There are four theme answers (not three as the columnist writes) and a revealer. I thought it was super clever! Kudos to the constructor, and I think his original clues mentioned in the constructor notes were much better than the substitutes created by the editing committee. (We all know that a camel is a horse designed by a committee!)

How to break your opponent’s serve at Wimbledon?


SOFTRETURN

Great puzzle, Mr. Keller! The trick, of course, once I caught on, was to fill in the theme answers without looking down at the QWERTY. There may be other ways of truing a bike wheel, but the method I learned was to pluck the spokes, not unlike Orpheus plucking his lyre, and adjust their tension, until the all sounded the same pitch. In other words, literally a "tune-up." It's a PITA, which is why I pay the technician at my LBS to do it for me. Here's various _characters_, all as portrayed by Elle Cordova, commenting on today's puzzle: https://www.youtube.com/shorts/ky0YOo7_Y0o This puzzle reminded me of one of my FOAT puzzles, which I believe was a Thurs. from 2020; I haven't figured out how to search for it on xwordinfo.com, or the like, but if I do, I will reply with a link to the archive.
What a fun puzzle. Great theme. When I figured it out, I refused to look for clues which wasn't easy if you knew my wpm.

Delightful Wednesday
  Thank you!

Seemed like a Tuesday puzzle to me, with a rather pedestrian "theme" to boot. And DOLLARSYMBOL rather than DOLLARSIGN struck me as very forced. Hoping for something more interesting tomorrow.

There were five K's in the grid, clustered in the SE corner.

It's a good thing I don't keep track of streaks, because I really, really blew this one. First, I solve on paper, far from my PC keyboard. I had no idea what was under the PLUS SIGN, but managed to get that from the crosses. Likewise, also no clue about the Guns N' Roses guitarist's stage name, but getting QUESTION MARK from the crosses made it unnecessary.

Where I totally screwed up was in the top middle. I'm usually quick to think of alternate meanings for clues, but couldn't get past the baseball thing to get SPIELS; I was trying to make a rebus out of Spitballs. Then I had no idea who the Game of Thrones character was. This could have all worked out, if only I'd thought of what I always did as a teenager, before my hippie phase kicked in, and that was TEASE my hair. And at some point I did know that it was back-combing. I am aghast at blowing a Wednesday puzzle, after congratulating myself just the other day on my lack of cognitive decline after breezing through the Friday and Saturday puzzles. Hope this isn't the beginning of a trend.

This is a very clever idea for a puzzle, but, alas, I didn't enjoy it at all.  I knew that every answer was going to be some sort of keyboard sign or symbol or punctuation mark, but I had no idea what or why.  I didn't even know the answers to the easier ones with numbers -- you think I remember what is over the 4 or the 9?

EQUALS and SLASH are even more subtle, and I didn't even get those clues in the first place, much less know where they were on the keyboard.  FWIW, I never heard of SLASH, the guitarist -- but after the fact, I Googled "Guitarist S" and SLASH came right up, even without Guns N' Roses.

As I said, clever and imaginative.  But where's the fun for me if, after the solve, I have to turn on a very strong light over my keyboard and stand up to see what's on those keys in the back?  (If I sit down, there's a glare and I can't see anything.)
So this didn't really work for me.  And I wasn't thrilled with the texting initials either.  I never am.

How strict are you about "cheating"?

Is it cheating to look at the keyboard while solving this puzzle?
Would it be cheating if you were solving the dead-tree edition to open your laptop to look?
This was clever and very fun to solve!

Well - tough one for me, but finally tumbled to the trick and that ended up being a huge turning point. Clever puzzle, no complaints. And... puzzle find(s) today - a bit surprised by this. 

First - a Friday from June 10, 1994 by Wayne Robert Williams. Three theme answers: 

QWERTYUIOP
ASDFGHJKL
ZXCVBNM

And then the reveal - 3 connected answers: 

ORDEROF
LETTERSON
TYPEWRITER

And then - a Wednesday from February 7, 2007 by David Kwong. 

Three theme answers on that one: 

QWERTYUIOP
ASDFGHJKL
ZXCVBNM

And.. the reveal(s)

TYPEWRITER
KEYBOARDS.

Hmmm... suppose it could be a coincidence, but... who knows. 

....
Delightful puzzle.  Always great to get something new and creative.  More of this please.

An Egyptian God in a crossword?  It's gotta be ISIS.  If it's not ISIS, then it's gotta be OSIRIS.  If it's neither of them, it's gotta be AMON-RA.
Right?  
Not today.

Later in life, I became fascinated with Egyptian canopic jars which contained the embalmed organs of those lucky enough to be mummified, in preparation for their next life.  The jars are usually topped with the image of a God, often ANUBIS, who is associated with the afterlife.

But I first became familiar with the jackal-headed deity ANUBIS through the dialogues of Plato.  Socrates frequently swears, "By the Dog, God of the Egyptians!"
If you're a careful reader of the dialogues, you should ask yourself why is this Greek man swearing by an Egyptian God?  If you fail to ask this question, I'm not interested in the rest of your interpretation of the dialogues.
great puzzle top right had us scrambling but we got there in the end

Agree with others.  Very well constructed and fun to solve. 

Thank you.
Totally adorable puzzle.

Already 150+ Comments....  so I read a few, and Nancy in NYC pretty much said it all. 

I'm hopeless when it comes to rock stars/bands, so .....Guns'n'Roses, bleah. 
I found the Reveal clue, with the hint about the SHIFT KEU...oops, I guess LUTE was wrong, so it's a LYRE.  
So, YEAH, I opened the iPad and looked at the Logitech KEYboard.
FYI, we all call it a DOLLAR SIGN.
I've never seen/read Game of Thrones, and you can't make me.  
What is "Broad City"?  Movie? TV show? Tacky nickname for a metropolis?  Maybe populated by people named SLASH and ANUBIS? And who own INU Shiba doggies?

Just sign me "Proud to be WOKE."

I love when I figure out a theme and genuinely laugh and smile - today was that day. Especially since I knew the "real" answer to 47A, and was just not connecting how it could possibly fit, then finding the revealer, I laughed out loud. Great puzzle, one of my favorites in a long time.
SPIELS got me. Excellent clueing.
I feel bad for users of other keyboard layouts.

Sorry to be a stickler, but while the column states that “Each theme clue, such as 24-Across [*Number of years between Summer Olympics], refers to a number”, that’s only the case for 2 of the themers- the others refer to a slash and an equal sign. Right?

My Shiba INU has given this puzzle his paw of approval. 
Took me a while to work out the trick, doesn’t help that the DOLLAR SIGN is not shift F here, but got there eventually.

🧵Hello, everyone, hope you will all have a fine day, despite the political carnage & cannibalism all around us, 🙄.

At first, I grokked (in the original meaning) that the Explainer had to do with the _____ ___, so I chose CAPS LOCK, 😉👋🏽!
Just as an interesting quirk, this only works if you have an English language keyboard.  The dollar sign on my Danish keyboard is together with the § sign and no number.
Indeed, NESTS belong in trees. Try telling that to the starlings, who insist on building theirs in my dryer vent.

Fun theme! The second one let me see what the game was. It was nice to use one of the more subtle themers first so as not to give it away right up front. I don't know the keyboard well but the crosses did the job. My first thought for [Kind of line at the beach] was Tow, but a glance at 51 D made TAN the obvious choice. Being a later in the day beach goer covered by a wetsuit with any remaining skin slathered with sun block, TAN is the last thing I think about. Ahead of his upcoming 84th birthday, Taj MAHAL is the artist of the day on my favorite radio station. Heard this morning, a nod to another NYT game: Queen Bee https://youtu.be/QCN4_dDysQ4?si=wL1ioT2WavEkOOWV

Perfect Wednesday -- once you realized the theme, the clues allowed a quicker solve - which is the goal. 

Well done.
That was fun!
A bit trickier on the phone where the shift key only gets you caps, but not as hard as on a non-American keyboard.
Great Wednesday puzzle!
Thank you, Brian, for such a clever grid -- it SHIFTed my thinking in the very best way. I look forward to seeing more from you!😊
Clever without being annoying!  Bravo!
Got them all, but I don't understand QUESTIONMARK. What does that have to do with Axl Rose?

Quite cool to have both AXLE as an answer in the same grid as a Guns n' Roses clue. 

Very clever theme and enjoyable solve. The only parts I struggled with were the NW corner as I had LYFT for a while instead of TAXI and couldn't figure out anything else.

I also couldn't get HES for "Bucks and bulls" until I got ABASE (I kept thinking HEN? HEW? HEM? until I realised I should've been conferring personhood to said animals). Otherwise, a fun puzzle.
Figured out the trick at the Guns n' Roses clue. (That tells you what generation I'm in.) Fun puzzle all around. Wednesday is my favorite. The Goldilocks of crosswords.
Didn't get the connection between the theme clues and answers until reading the summary.  Cleverly done.
Wonderful puzzle and almost had to check against a real keyboard!
Who put the Thursday puzzle up early?

One more quite amazing puzzle find. A Sunday from August 8, 1982 by Mary Virginia Orna with the title: "Latin Rhythms." Six 23 letter grid-spanning theme answers: "Hit song of 1914?" BYTHEPULCHRITUDINOUSSEA And all the rest were similarly clued (by year) DANCINGINTHETENEBROSITY YOULLNEVERAMBULATEALONE MYOLDKENTUCKYHABITATION IVEGOTYOUSUBCUTANEOUSLY ILLSEEYOUINMYPHANTASMAS Here's that link: https://www.xwordinfo.com/PS?date=8/8/1982&g=82&d=A ....
Loved this one, even though PEERS had me scratching my head for a good while.
I enjoyed today's puzzle, already excited for the next one.
Possibly a record solve for me today, only slowed down by NEKE for 35D (I am of the opinion that ( is "open parentheses", not the singular). Had an AHA moment in the end.
33D: Perhaps, but ask for their preferred pronouns first. 🙃

Congrats on a fine puzzle, Mr. Keller! Excellent theme, and I loved your clueing (95 Shift 5?!)

My son supposedly made pizza for Slash once. We'll never know if it's true, but that's what the person picking up the order said. We like to believe it's true.

Someone snuck a fine Tuesday puzzle into Wednesday (did this one 32.5% faster than my rolling average according to XWStats).

I didn't even have to read the theme clues until the puzzle was filled in, because the answers were readily apparent from the crosses.
Great theme and I loved the execution. NIKE being clued as “Personification of victory” is a little dubious imo…

For anyone still thinking about replying to @Elie L's survey link yesterday about streaks..you snooze you lose. I lost.

I was going to say I think chasing streaks is a bad idea. But to each their own.

Nice one 🤓. I also solve on a phone so had no extra help with the keyboard layout. The only one I knew from memory was that the $ was shift-4. But after figuring out the theme it wasn’t too difficult to fill in the themers from a smattering of letters. 

For my own personal puzzle solving rules, it would have been “cheating” to look at full keyboard. But we all have our own way of doing things and YMMV. I won’t Google anything, but I’m happy to ask a family member about a clue I think is in their wheelhouse. However, if they don’t know it and they decide to look it up, I hastily remind them not to tell me the answer in that case. 🤓
Now I gotta know Tik Tok jargon? Great.
Such a fun puzzle. Loved it!! 👏🏽👏🏽👏🏽

This was such a satisfying solve because I had to work REALLY hard to get the upper left corner and it involved searching my keyboard.  I had LYFT for 1A and YOKE for 2D and ROYAL for 6D which gave me *K**S*Y* for *PEERS, which I knew had to be one of the upper (shifted) symbols above a key.  First step was to replace ROYAL with REGAL which gave me *K**S*G*  and then to realize that S*G* had to be SIGN. So then I had *K**SIGN, and that meant the K was wrong.  LYFT hadnt seemed to work from the start so I got rid of YOKE and LYFT.  Then I stared at the keyboard to find something that could be PEERS, and oh the delightful aha when I realized it was the symbol you shift to get the PLUS SIGN:  EQUALS.

From there it was easy to fill in the rest of the top left section.

Ive been solving the NYT crossword for years and somehow constructors never run out of ideas!  This one was delightful.

I loved this fun puzzle with a clever theme! 

I was today’s years old when I learned: PARENTHESES is the plural form of Parenthesis! This caused me a problem in the eastern side as I was pretty sure NIKE was the personification of Victory but didn’t fit with PARENTHESES… 

I really appreciate themes that actually help you solve a puzzle. Too often themes have no bearing on the puzzle or only makes sense once you finish the puzzle and read the cross play article.

Rating:
9/10

Oof I was going nuts in the top corner. TAXI was what I needed to get things going thanks to a comment. Entire puzzle was solved, I even had ____SIGN filled out.

Awesome puzzle. I had a lot of fun with this one. I know people complain about the difficulty getting much easier, but really this was just at my skill level. After a year of this, the only puzzles I can’t solve without lookups are Fridays and Saturdays.

One more puzzle - a Sunday from July 17, 1988 by Norman S. Wizer with the title "Versified Versions." Five 21 letter grid-spanning theme answers in that one: LEWDBROODESCHEWEDFEUD EIGHTAWAITGREATDEBATE WISEGUYSCRITICIZELIES GEESEFLEECEOBESENIECE AIDEDELAYEDSTAIDBLADE Here's the link: https://www.xwordinfo.com/PS?date=7/17/1988&g=22&d=A ....

Rather late on this but I had to put the puzzle down for a while after checking and checking to find my mistake. I kept thinking YESH was not a solve for [Opposite of “nah”]…it just didn’t seem right, but I was darn sure CHINS was a great solve for [Some porcelain pieces “] somehow thinking taking one on the chin involved a fragile subset of chins.  Oh well…
I'm old, I drove a cab for a living while I was floundering around after grad school, I don't have a single ride-share app, and I still tried "Lyft" first.

Today's poem made from words found in today's puzzle

a/ the beauty of what
the supreme what
the what you’d need
the answers
to what it was
was it d/ imagined?
i imagined a map of tree houses
majestic tree houses
with fur for sleeping on
the important
a/ question

Regarding 43A, I present to you Brooklyn 99's finest: 

Captain Holt: I must say, this is going considerably better than when I came out to my colleagues. They were not, as the kids say, awake.

Jake: Do you mean woke?

Captain Holt: I did mean woke. But it's grammatically incoherent.

Bravo Brian, I have to admit I don't always get Wednesday puzzles but when I parsed together DOLLAR SIGN = 4 I was thrilled. One small comment. One answer was ERDOCS which is fine, but for many years the Emergency Room has been called Emergency Department! David Gropper, MD
Hated it. Made no sense. I don't want to have to read an article to solve a puzzle.

OK - and one more puzzle find. I'll re-post this tomorrow. A Sunday from November 4, 1979 by William Lutwiniak with the title "Dual Roles." A couple of theme clue and answer examples: "Ex-quarterback turns numbers man." JOENAMATHEMATICIAN "Actor plays Saarinen." FREDRICMARCHITECT And some other theme answers: DONAMECHESSMASTER FRANKSINATRASHMAN RICHARDBASEHARTIST ISAACASIMOVIESTAR ARNOLDPALMERCHANT EDWARDALBEEKEEPER Here's that link: https://www.xwordinfo.com/PS?date=11/4/1979&g=84&d=A ....
This had to be the sparsest Wordplay column I've ever seen....
Fun puzzle today. Got it all with no look-ups and without reading the Gameplay column first. Very happy when I got the keyboard trick. Yay!
The NW corner stressed me the XOUT out! 😰

I got got by the NW corner sadly. I will betray my ignorance by admitting I had no idea what an AXON is and I seem to have gone blind on INS. Just couldn't see it. The rest of the puzzle was really fun. Love all the Xs and Ks.
It’s not Thursday! I’m still doing this, and might quit.

This was anything easy solved, and a cute theme. My only nit pick here is that plus would have made more sense than plus sign, since the answer is equal, and not equal sign.  Open paren and question mark make sense.

Otherwise, a fun solve.
https://youtu.be/0cHSYhafCQM?si=36MaqS5e3aoMWkPU Enjoy!
This is going to really annoy the Brits when they wake up. I actually solved this with pretty much no issues (aside from a foolish extra S in dreams vs dreamt) I must be getting better.
The comments are gone from Thursday’s puzzle. Anyone else having the same experience?
@Dan take a peek at a keyboard

@Steve L
That's why I never thought I'd be first!

DYT,
You were nosed out by someone 
who had filled in the puzzle 
but had not yet solved it.

@Steve L

As a kid, I couldn't wait until the next episode of Get Smart; I thought they were hilarious.  The Cone of Silence.  Sorry about that, Chief.  And...loving it.  Would you believe...  The Maxisms made me laugh every time.

Seeing an episode 50 years later, well, not so much.  I don't think their target audience was 8-year-old boys, so I guess comedy has just changed over the years.

And, amid current events, one might want to remember that max was Agent 86, a number chosen because it was common slang for something that should be removed, not something more odious.

@Barry Ancona 

I prefer the Webster's 
definition of solve, but
I get what you were
trying to say.

The editors are inviting a deluge of objections to finding stuff out and then changing your attitudes based on what you learned.

userLocation: Spring Hill, just south of Armageddon

commentBody: @Barry Ancona son of an ER nurse and partner to one, and I was miffed by this clue, especially as Nurses Week just ended!

userLocation: Spring Hill, just south of Armageddon
commentBody:
@lucky13

We think we're pretty valuable, too.

(To be fair, though, ED docs don't "put together" anyone.  We stabilize.)

But we're constantly dumped on by our other-specialty colleagues who still often tend to look down on us as the handymen or jack-of-all-trades of medicine and routinely complain about the ignorance of our decision making and handling of patients.

The internists don't think we know enough medicine.
 
The surgeons don't think we understand enough about surgery.

The pediatricians...the orthopedists...etc.

Hi Matt,
A good friend, npw nearing retirement age, chose Emergency Medicine, and has practiced it for decades at a "Hosp." in the U.S. I think he would agree with you about the "no respect." I don't think he would call himself a "triage specialist."

userLocation: Spring Hill, just south of Armageddon
commentBody:
@Barry Ancona

I would never call what we do "triage" work.

I think a good way to think of our specialty is: start with a GP of old or a rural family doc now and add 10 or 15 lifesaving skills to his arsenal.

And I've known tons of ED docs whose judgment is frequently better than the specialists'.

In the end, as with most things, your title has nothing to do with how good you are.

Matt,
Amen.

Matt,
But as you said elsewhere in this thread, "I would never call what we do 'triage' work."

userLocation: Spring Hill, just south of Armageddon
commentBody:
@Barry Ancona

Correct.

Only when the sky's falling.

And it's a gruesome sort of triage:  in what order to care for victims and who's beyond help.

The old film and show MASH, in my opinion, did a better job of showing what this is like than any other program/film before or since that I've seen.

Matt,
The second time I saw M*A*S*H was at the 93rd Evac on Long Binh Post. Talk about meta.

userLocation: Spring Hill, just south of Armageddon
commentBody:
@Ms. Billie M. Spaight

As we say around these parts:  Oy.

I'm just funnin' my pal Andrzej.

Truth be told I'm often quite sad I didn't become a classicist or a logician or a linguist who wiles away his days and flagging energies on some campus somewhere instead of engaging in the Sisyphean and absurd task of trying to fix breaking-down human bodies.

@Andrzej  That's fine--you don't have to care, and you certainly don't have to be delighted.  But equally, I have a right to comment when someone else's post strikes me as both illogical and unfair.  The people who put these puzzles together put a LOT of work into them.  Saying that their whole idea was bad just because you couldn't easily find the answer by looking at your keyboard seemed both ridiculous and unfair, so in fairness to the creator, I felt a need to point that out.  (It's not as if we had to press the shift key to get the answer.  Nothing changes if we do.)

@M. Hogan

I really do not understand how you refuse to see the problem: if you are sitting at a computer, you can instantly check the answers to the themed clues. If you are using another device, you can't. Thus, on a phone, you have to get the themed entries from crosses. How does this *not* mean that the theme doesn't really work on any other device than a computer? The puzzle is still solvable, sure, but the experience of solving on a computer and on another device is materially different, and the details of the theme are much less relevant in the latter case (so it doesn't really work). What is unfair and ridiculous here? It's simply the truth. If anything, refusing to accept it seems ridiculous to me. You are apparently prepared to turn a blind eye to the theme's defficiencies, while I am not.

Also, I can't see how it makes sense to formulate personal opinions of anything based on the effort put into whatever it is we are opining on. Many hard endeavors result in sub-par outcomes, and how hard it was to get there in no way makes the end result any better. 

Feel free to keep on riding the big D of delight, just don't expect me to hop on, too.
@Andrzej Duly noted

@Andrzej   I'm not refusing to see the problem, Andrzej.  I understand that it would have been easier to solve the theme clues if we'd using our computers instead of our phones. (And it was still easier on the phone than it would have been if we'd been sitting in a subway car or somewhere else out of phone reach with the folded paper, and no way to check what the top bar of a keyboard looks like.)  But the puzzle was still solvable, and there wasn't any special effect that we missed out on, so I really don't understand what makes you think this puzzle shouldn't even have been created.

@M. Hogan

Do not put words into my mouth. I never said the puzzle should not have been created. I just opened the theme was faulty for reasons stated above.

Stoat is just another name for ermine. They’re the same animal. I suspect “ermine” is the preferred name when referring to the fur because a stoat coat just doesn’t sound as elegant.

They're two names for the same animal. However, in the winter, the coat of the animal becomes more snowy white--for camouflage. It is the winter coat that is more prized and is used in ceremonial garb: it is more likely to be called ermine.

commentBody: @Jane Wheelaghan I knew of Slash but not ABBI or ULTA. SO had difficulties in the same part of the grid as you. I eventually tried TSA BIN because I couldn't think of anything else and have done enough NYT xwords to know of TSA as common filer.

@Jane Wheelaghan
In case you haven’t already looked it up for yourself, TCBY is a US nationwide chain of stores selling soft-serve frozen yogurt. It started in Little Rock, Arkansas, in the early 1980s and quickly expanded franchise operations across most of the country. (I was living in Little Rock at the time and had met the gentleman who started the company,) The abbreviation originally meant “This Can’t Be Yogurt,” but a competing chain named “I Can’t Believe It’s Yogurt,” which had been in existence a few years, sued over copyright infringement. The abbreviation meaning was changed to “The Country’s Best Yogurt.”

@Mark Smith
Thanks, I can't remember it coming up in previous crosswords. I've never had frozen yogurt. I expect it's available but I've not seen it advertised.
@Jane Wheelaghan I didn't know / either. Never heard of him. I've seen quarts of ice cream and definitely milk. Quart bottles and then containers.
@Mark Smith It's not here in NYC.

@Charles Peterson
Yes! 
I’ve never used quarts at all. Remember that our pints are bigger than yours - we are 20 fluid ounces and you have 16 ounces. Nobody ever used fluid ounces though.

Although it’s all litres now. Wine is in mils.

@Jeff Nike is the Greek goddess of victory
@Jeff. The running shoe is named after NIKE the Greek goddess of victory. There is a statue of her in the Louvre

@Jeff When I was growing up in the Chicago suburbs, there was a Nike base within walking distance of my parents' home. This one: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Arlington_Heights_Army_Air_Defense_Site Nike bases were Nike missile sites, operated by the Army Air Defense Command, relics of the cold war. Full (long!) article and listing here: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/List_of_Nike_missile_sites Somewhat off-topic, I realize, but for me, almost half the fun of doing NYT Crosswords is diving down the rabbit holes offered by obscure clues and reader comments. These two are my offering du jour. Also, the marching band of men from the Nike Base were always the highlight of the Independence Day Parade. Shiny helmets and very loud drums!

@Sonja
I find different keyboard layouts fascinating! How does the Finnish one differ from our English (US) one?

I have three different ones loaded onto my laptop:
1) the standard English-US which came with set-up
2) English-US-International, which is what I have the PC set to at most times. It makes it easy to type things like "crème brûlée" with ease. It covers all of the Western European languages, including the Scandinavian ones, but falls short on (Roman-alphabet) Slavic ones, and Hungarian. Also, it is "a bit of a pain" to remember to his the space bar after hitting the quotes key, or else you'll get ä bit of a pain."
3) Something called Ält-Latin 2019 (whoops! I meant "Alt-Latin 2019"), which covers those. I don't use it often enough so when I do, I have to resort to a cheat-sheet. And it still doesn't cover that dot-less ï"--I mean "i"--one finds in Turkish.

Ironic one can not type "Dvořák" on a standard English DVORAK keyboard.

@Sonja
My father's old boss, who fled East Germany as the wall was going up, had a typewriter with umlaut vowels. I thought that was totally cool.

@Bill the Finnish keyboard has a few extra keys on the right for ä, ö and å, and then the symbols are at different places. And, for example, Serbian quertz keyboards are almost the same, but have z and y switched, for some reason. I don't think they have specific keys for out ć/č/š/etc. I haven't had a laptop with a Serbian keyboard (I'm Serbian but have moved to Finland 10 years ago), so I'm not sure about the symbols.

Another funny story, so Serbian can be written with both the Latin and Cyrillic alphabet, and most people I know just use the Latin one when typing, many also when writing by hand. But all official documents must be written in Cyrillic letters. Do you know how difficult it was to write my high school thesis in Cyrillics on a Latin keyboard?? Especially because some letters look the same, but are different letters, like H (which is actually N in Serbian,) or C (S). I had to use muscle memory and not look at the keyboard because then I would mess up.

@Grant that is brilliant! My dog inherited a toy from my friend's old family dog that has a label that says "Made in East Germany"
Fun relics from the times past!

@Call Me Al Ooh, ooh! Sixteen minutes in and three quarters of a cup of coffee and I cracked the theme!

Clever idea! Let's go!
@Call Me Al  Twenty-eight and change. Not bad for me on a Wednesday. Needed a couple of Google checks and no clue as to the GnR guitarist.
@Call Me Al Ha, ha. I thought Lyft too because that's direct competition of a rideshare company. But not to give you a spoiler, the answer is different.
@CCNY: Andy, smarty pants, did anyone ever ask you why they called a standard bottle of liquor a "fifth"?

@CCNY
Before age 8 I had to recite:
4 gills one pint
2 pints one quart
4 quarts one gallon

Much later I found out that whisky was measured in fractions of a gill, although now it's mls. I never found a use for QUART.

@Don H

Not smart! Too young to overthink it!

And a fifth? 
A fifth of a what? A gallon..?

Okay. I just looked it up.
Now I know *two* things!

Thank you Don H!

@Jane Wheelaghan

My husband and I owned
an ice cream parlo(u)r for many years.

It’s a very solid way to learn
pints, quarts, gallons…

But toss gills and mls at me
and I will properly fail every time.

@Don H

Because "750 milliliters" is too much of a mouthful?

@CCNY
I wish ice cream still came in 2-qt containers.  Now they are only 1.5 quarts.  And what used to be a pint is only 12 oz.

It's the sneaky price inflation.  Don't raise the price -- just make the amount smaller.

Tuna used to be in 8 oz cans.  Now the same cans hold 5.5 oz.

But the worst is sweetend condensed milk.  I have a wonderful lemon meringue pie recipe that calls for 16 oz.  That's what used to be in a can.  Now the cans are 14 oz, so I have to use a whole one and part of another, or it doesn't work with the amount of lemon juice and eggs.   I can't do 7/8 of an egg!

@CCNY,

I ❤️ this story.

It is said that after death, ANUBIS would weigh the heart of the deceased in a scale against a feather.  If the heart was heavier than the feather, it would be devoured by Ammit.  If it was lighter, the soul would be admitted to the next world.

"If you fail to ask this question, I'm not interested in the rest of your interpretation of the dialogues."

The X-Phile,
Not exactly a Socratic approach!

@Barry Ancona

On the other hand, I'd pay almost anything to sit by Socrates and hear him interpret one of Plato's dialogues.

@Barry Ancona

Although I've never met the man, I'd wager a considerable amount that Socrates would not spend any time reading secondary literature about the dialogues of Plato.

@Barry Ancona - Not to open Pandora's handbag or anything, but I'm seriously lacking in my understanding of Greek mythology. It's sort of my Achilles wrist. 

Anyway, I've been raining to ask, so I'll digress right now...  I am a woodworker and I recently noticed that a writer for Wood magazine is Vincent Ancona. Any relation?

Captain Q,
Nope.
So why is Socrates swearing by an Egyptian God?

@Convoid-04

There are many possible answers, none of which are certain, all of which should make one question and think more.

One might start by asking whether Socrates questions the "truth" of the Greek Gods.
One might wonder why "our" Gods are different from "theirs", and ask whether one can come to any rational understanding of the Gods and their nature.
One might speculate that Socrates is saying that his thinking is not limited to the borders of ancient Athens.  Philosophy is more universal than religion.

But, primarily, one needs to question the tradition that was handed down and learn to think for oneself.

@Mean Old Lady
I do. Own a Shiba INU that is, though as INU is Dog in Japanese he’s just a Shiba dog.  I adore him, but don’t recommend the breed for a first time owner; they are the cats of the dog world and as such treat their human serfs with the disdain they clearly deserve, that is when they’re not trying to maul the postman/delivery driver/visitor who looked at him funny.

We’re old hands at dog owning, so sniffed at the advice not to be a Shiba family if you haven’t had dogs before. They are demons cunningly disguised as adorable fluff balls. commentBody:
@Emma

There's a comment above that says PLUSSIGN is the shifted version of the = key. PEERS, EQUALS get it?
@Emma had to look at the physical object to get that one.

@Emma "Peers" can be a clue for EQUALS (two peers in a discipline, of "equal" skill), and of course Shift with the Equal key gives you a PLUS SIGN. 

It's a twice-removed clue, which was too tricky for me to sort out without lots of fill first.
@Emma wouldn't worry - sat in my office scanning my keyboard and literally said "oh!!!" outloud

A parenthesis is a punctuation mark used to enclose information, similar to a bracket. The open parenthesis, which looks like (, is used to begin parenthetical text. The close parenthesis, ), denotes the end of parenthetical text. The plural of parenthesis is parentheses.
